title: Build Like a Pro

lessons.title: Build Pages Like a Professional (Semantic HTML)

accomplishments: Transform a basic page into a professionally structured page
Understand WHY structure matters for accessibility
Make your code readable and SEO-friendly

do.title: Transform divs with semantic tags
steps: Notice: it looks the same but the code has meaning now

do.result: Now the code has semantic meaning. Screen readers can understand your structure!
do.proofRequired: your code showing the semantic tags (<header>, <nav>, <main>, <footer>)

concepts.name: <header> tag
concepts.definition: Top of page or section. Contains site title, logo, tagline, or article metadata (title, author, date).
concepts.analogy: The letterhead on official paper - identifies what document this is.

concepts.name: <nav> tag
concepts.definition: Navigation links. Screen readers can jump straight to navigation with one keystroke!
concepts.analogy: The table of contents in a book - helps you find different sections.

concepts.name: <main> tag
concepts.definition: Primary content. Only ONE per page. This is THE content people came for. Screen readers can skip straight here!
concepts.analogy: The article in a magazine - not the ads, not the index, just the story.

concepts.name: <article> tag
concepts.definition: Self-contained content. Test: Could this stand alone if copied to another site? If YES → use <article>.
concepts.analogy: A newspaper article you could cut out and share independently.

concepts.name: <section> tag
concepts.definition: Thematic grouping of content. Each section should have a heading.
concepts.analogy: Chapters in a book - different themes, different sections.

concepts.name: <aside> tag
concepts.definition: Related but not main content. Sidebars, pull quotes, related links, fun fact boxes.
concepts.analogy: Sidebar notes in a textbook - related info but not the main lesson.

concepts.name: <footer> tag
concepts.definition: Bottom of page or section. Copyright info, contact details, social links, site map.
concepts.analogy: The fine print at the bottom of a contract.

understand.keyTakeaway: Use the RIGHT tag for the job. Semantic HTML makes your code accessible to screen readers and understandable to search engines.

build.goal: Build a complete semantic page using ALL the semantic tags

Site Title
Links

build.hint: You can have multiple headers and footers (page header, article header, etc.) but only ONE <main> per page

challenge.title: Restructure Your Portfolio Site
challenge.mission: Go back to your existing pages and replace all divs with proper semantic tags.
requirements: Wrap your navigation in <nav> with a list
If you have blog posts or projects, use <article>
Ensure heading hierarchy is correct (h1 → h2 → h3, no skipping)

challenge.bonusChallenge: Add <time> tags around dates with proper 'datetime' attributes, and use <mark> to highlight important text

capabilities: Use <header> for page and section headers
Understand why semantic HTML matters for accessibility
Write code that screen readers can understand

bridge.preview: In Lesson 8, you'll learn text formatting (<strong>, <em>, <mark>), special characters, and developer tools for debugging your code.
